revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:白山神社 (新潟市中央区一番堀通町)]]


{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]
| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]
| image                 = File:Hakusan Shrine (Niigata City) Haiden.jpg
| caption = The shrine
| location = {{ill|Ichibanboridōri-chō|qid=Q17193259|1=Ichibanboridōri-chō}}
| coordinates = {{Coord|37.915611111111|N|139.03733333333|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}
| deity = {{ill|Izanagi and Izanami|qid=Q12259961|1=Izanagi and Izanami}}; {{ill|Kikurihime|qid=Q11619891|1=Kikurihime}}; {{ill|Shirayama Hime Shrine|qid=Q704702|1=Shirayama Hime Shrine}}
}}
{{ill|Hakusan Shrine|ja|白山神社_(新潟市中央区一番堀通町)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakusan Shrine|qq=}} is a [[Beppyo Shrine]].<ref>https://www.visiting-japan.com/en/articles/tokyo/e13bu-hakusan-jinja.htm</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=https://old-tokyo.info/hakusan-shrine-local-shrine-full-history-hydrangeas/|title=Hakusan shrine: local shrine full of history (with hydrangeas)|date=November 22, 2017}}</ref> it worships the [[Chinjugami]] of {{ill|Niigata Prefecture|en|Niigata Prefecture|qid=Q132705|lt=Niigata Prefecture|1=Niigata Prefecture}}.

== History ==
The exact date of the shrine's establishment is unknown due to the loss of old records in two fires during the {{ill|Eiroku|en|Eiroku|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eiroku|qq=}} (1558–) and {{ill|Tensho|en|Tenshō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tensho|qq=}} (1573–) periods<ref name="origin"/>. However, it is said to have been founded in the {{ill|Engi|en|Engi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engi|qq=}} (901–) or {{ill|Kanji (era)|en|Kanji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanji (era)|qq=}} (1087–) periods<ref name="origin"/>. Additionally, records show that in the {{ill|Genki|en|Genki|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Genki|qq=}} period (1570–), Kenkai, the chief priest of {{ill|Hōki-in (Niigata)|en|Hōki-in (Niigata)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōki-in (Niigata)|qq=}}, which was the head temple of Hakusan Shrine, enshrined a sacred sword, a sacred mirror, and a bronze statue of the {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|en|Ekādaśamukha|qid=Q11404731|lt=Eleven-Faced Kannon|1=Ekādaśamukha}} at Hakusan Shrine<ref name="origin"/>. This statue of the {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|en|Ekādaśamukha|qid=Q11404731|lt=Eleven-Faced Kannon|1=Ekādaśamukha}} was returned to Hōki-in during the separation of Shinto and Buddhism in the first year of {{ill|Meiji|en|Meiji period|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji|qq=}}<ref>Located at 1612-1 Nishi Horidori 9-chome, Chuo Ward, Niigata City.</ref> and still exists today<ref name="origin">[https://www.niigatahakusanjinja.or.jp/about/yuisho.html The history of Hakusan Shrine] Hakusan Shrine official site, accessed June 28, 2022.</ref>.

During the {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|qq=}}, {{ill|Uesugi Kagekatsu|en|Uesugi Kagekatsu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uesugi Kagekatsu|qq=}} made significant offerings to the shrine following his military victories<ref name="origin"/>.

In Meiji 6 (1873), when the Meiji government issued a decree for the establishment of a park ({{ill|Hakusan Park (Niigata)|en|Hakusan Park (Niigata)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakusan Park (Niigata)|qq=}}), the auxiliary and branch shrines within the pine forest of the shrine grounds were merged into the main shrine to create the park. This resulted in the enshrinement of many deities<ref name="origin"/>.

The shrine was historically listed as a Shikinaisha and is mentioned in the "{{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|en|Engishiki|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}} Jinmyocho" as "Funae Shrine" in the Numata District of Echigo Province<ref name="origin"/>. In {{ill|Taisho|en|Taishō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taisho|qq=}} 13 (1924), it was promoted to a prefectural shrine<ref name="origin"/> and in {{ill|Showa|en|Shōwa period|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Showa|qq=}} 56 (1981), it was elevated to the Rank of Beppyo Shrine<ref name="origin"/>.
